items.subtopic: Meningococcal Infections | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY AND MICROBIOLOGY
items.title: Understanding Meningococcal Disease Presentation and Carriage
items.content: Neisseria meningitidis primarily colonizes the nasopharynx asymptomatically, but can lead to invasive diseases such as bacterial meningitis or septicemia. Other presentations include occult bacteremia, pneumonia, septic arthritis, conjunctivitis, and chronic meningococcemia.
keyPoints: Asymptomatic colonization is common in healthy adolescents and adults.
Invasive disease presents as bacterial meningitis or meningococcal septicemia.
Other presentations include occult bacteremia, pneumonia, septic arthritis, conjunctivitis, and chronic meningococcemia.
items.reference: Meningococcal Infections · Meningococcal Infections | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY AND MICROBIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Meningococcal Infections | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY AND MICROBIOLOGY
items.title: Capsular Groups and Disease Association in Meningococcus
items.content: The antigenic nature of the meningococcal capsule determines its capsular group, with six groups (A, B, C, X, Y, W) responsible for most invasive disease cases. Capsule-null variants are rare in invasive disease.
keyPoints: 12 capsular groups identified: A-C, X-Z, E, W, H-J, L.
Groups A, B, C, X, Y, and W account for the majority of invasive disease cases.
Capsule-null variants are rarely associated with invasive disease.
items.reference: Meningococcal Infections · Meningococcal Infections | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY AND MICROBIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Meningococcal Infections | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY AND MICROBIOLOGY
items.title: Genetic Typing and Adaptation in Meningococcus
items.content: Hyperinvasive lineages of N. meningitidis, characterized by genetic stability and adaptation to the nasopharyngeal environment, are responsible for most invasive cases globally. Genomic tools like MLST and whole genome sequencing aid in typing.
keyPoints: Hyperinvasive lineages persist over decades and cause most invasive disease.
Genetic stability indicates adaptation to host environments.
MLST and whole genome sequencing are used for meningococcal typing.
items.reference: Meningococcal Infections · Meningococcal Infections | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY AND MICROBIOLOGY

items.title: Patterns and Impact of Meningococcal Disease
items.content: Meningococcal disease occurs in various patterns including epidemic, outbreak, hyperendemic, and sporadic. The global incidence has been declining due to immunization efforts.
keyPoints: Up to 500,000 cases worldwide annually with a decline due to vaccination.
10% mortality rate among affected individuals.
Epidemics can reach rates as high as 1000 cases per 100,000 population in sub-Saharan Africa.
65% of outbreaks in the meningitis belt (2010-2017) were caused by capsular group C and 35% by W after immunization against A.

items.title: Epidemic Patterns and Clonal Associations
items.content: Epidemics often occur in specific regions like the sub-Saharan meningitis belt, with capsular group A historically predominant but now groups C and W are more common due to vaccination.
keyPoints: Sub-Saharan Africa experiences high rates of meningococcal disease, especially from capsular groups A, C, W, X.
Capsular group A epidemics occurred post-World Wars in Europe and North America.
Sequence type 11 clone linked with capsular groups C or W is associated with clusters in closed communities.

items.title: Regional Variations and Immunization Impact
items.content: The incidence of meningococcal disease varies by region, influenced by vaccination campaigns. The introduction of vaccines has significantly reduced cases in many areas.
keyPoints: U.S. rates fell from 1.2 to 0.06 per 100,000 (1997-2021).
Group B was predominant in U.S. children <5 years; groups C, W, Y in older age groups (2011-2021).
UK mass immunization against capsular group C starting in 1999 led to a shift with group B becoming predominant.
Introduction of MenB vaccine in the UK in 2015 reduced group B cases significantly.

items.title: Age and Genetic Factors in Meningococcal Disease Susceptibility
items.content: The susceptibility to meningococcal disease is primarily determined by age, with the highest incidence occurring during the first year of life due to lack of specific adaptive immunity. Adolescents and young adults also show a peak in disease incidence. Complement deficiency significantly increases the risk for invasive meningococcal disease.
keyPoints: Peak incidence occurs in the first year of life, with >30% of capsular group B cases occurring during this period in the United States.
Complement deficiency (C5–9, properdin, or factor D) increases risk by up to 600-fold and is found in only 0.3% of individuals with meningococcal disease.
7-20% of persons with disease from less common capsular groups (W, X, Y, Z, E) have a complement deficiency.

items.title: Impact of Social and Environmental Factors on Meningococcal Transmission
items.content: Social behaviors and environmental exposures significantly influence the transmission of N. meningitidis, with increased risk associated with close contact settings such as overcrowding, military recruitment, and certain social activities.
keyPoints: Close contact in crowded environments (e.g., refugee camps, college dormitories) increases transmission risk.
Secondary cases among close contacts can have a risk up to 1000 times the background rate.
Tobacco smoking has an odds ratio of 4.1 for increasing colonization and invasive disease risk.

items.title: Effects of Pandemic-Induced Behavioral Changes on Meningococcal Disease Rates
items.content: The COVID-19 pandemic led to reduced interpersonal contact, contributing to a substantial decline in meningococcal disease rates. These altered contact patterns may have a lasting impact on transmission dynamics.
keyPoints: Reductions in contacts during the COVID-19 pandemic resulted in significant declines in meningococcal disease cases.

items.title: Understanding Meningococcal Carriage Dynamics
items.content: N. meningitidis is a common colonizer of the human nasopharynx, with carriage rates exceeding 25% in adolescents and young adults, particularly in crowded communities. The variation in carriage rates among different meningococcal types suggests adaptations for either frequent transmission or prolonged colonization.
keyPoints: Asymptomatic infection rates >25% in some adolescent and young adult populations.
Carriage is rare in early childhood and declines to ~10% in adults.
Adaptations include short-duration carriage with frequent transmission or long-term colonization.

items.title: Mechanisms of Meningococcal Invasion
items.content: Invasion from the nasopharynx to the bloodstream is rare and usually occurs within days after acquiring an invasive strain. The meningococcal capsule is a critical virulence factor, providing resistance to phagocytosis and aiding in transmission.
keyPoints: Invasion through mucosa into bloodstream is rare, typically occurring within days of exposure.
The meningococcal capsule is crucial for resisting phagocytosis and preventing desiccation during host-to-host transmission.

items.title: Pathophysiology of Meningococcal Septicemia
items.content: Meningococcal septicemia involves a severe inflammatory response triggered by endotoxin, leading to endothelial injury and systemic effects such as capillary leak syndrome, intravascular coagulation, and shock.
keyPoints: Endotoxin initiates an inflammatory cascade releasing cytokines like TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6, IL-8, IL-10, PAI-1.
Endothelial injury causes increased vascular permeability, intravascular coagulation, and myocardial dysfunction.
Shock results from hypovolemia due to capillary leak syndrome, myocardial depression, and decreased tissue perfusion.

items.title: Pathogenesis and Severity of Meningococcal Infection
items.content: Meningococcal bacteria trigger a local inflammatory response in the meninges, releasing cytokines similar to those seen in septicemia. This inflammation is pivotal in determining the severity of neuronal injury, with potential for rapid onset of raised intracranial pressure due to cerebral edema and endothelial injury.
keyPoints: Cytokine release in meningitis mirrors that in septicemia.
Endothelial injury can cause cerebral edema and increased intracranial pressure.

items.title: Clinical Presentation of Meningococcal Disease
items.content: Meningococcal disease can manifest as meningitis or septicemia, with symptoms developing within 1–10 days post-acquisition. Common initial symptoms include fever, vomiting, and irritability, especially in infants. A nonblanching rash appears in >80% of cases but may be absent early on.
keyPoints: Symptoms develop 1–10 days after acquiring the organism.
>80% of cases present with a nonblanching rash (petechial or purpuric).
Initial symptoms include fever, vomiting, and irritability.

items.title: Meningococcal Meningitis and Septicemia
items.content: Meningococcal meningitis often presents with non-specific symptoms like fever and irritability, while septicemia can progress rapidly from nonspecific symptoms to death. Key signs of severe meningitis include raised intracranial pressure and focal neurologic signs, whereas septicemia may present with shock and multiorgan failure.
keyPoints: Meningitis presents with non-specific symptoms; >50% have associated septicemia features.
Severe meningitis can lead to raised intracranial pressure and death.
Septicemia progresses rapidly, potentially leading to shock and multiorgan failure.

items.title: Chronic Meningococcemia: Recognizing and Differentiating
items.content: Chronic meningococcemia presents with repeated episodes of petechial rash, fever, joint pain, arthritis features, and splenomegaly. It can progress to acute septicemia if untreated. The condition may be associated with complement deficiencies or inadequate sulfonamide therapy.
keyPoints: Differential diagnosis includes bacterial endocarditis, acute rheumatic fever, Henoch-Schönlein purpura, infectious mononucleosis, disseminated gonococcal infection, and immune-mediated vasculitis.
A study found half of isolates had an lpxL1 gene mutation leading to underacylated lipid A, reducing the inflammatory response.

items.title: Postmeningococcal Reactive Disease: Identification and Management
items.content: A small proportion of patients develop postmeningococcal reactive disease ~4–10 days after meningococcal disease onset, characterized by immune complex disease with rash, arthritis, iritis, pericarditis, and polyserositis. Recognizing this condition is crucial to avoid unnecessary prolonged antibiotic treatment.
keyPoints: Manifestations include a maculopapular or vasculitic rash (2% of cases), arthritis (up to 8% of cases), and iritis (1%).
The immune complexes involve meningococcal polysaccharide antigen, leading to spontaneous resolution without sequelae.

items.title: Diagnosis of Meningococcal Disease: Laboratory and Clinical Approaches
items.content: Meningococcal disease diagnosis involves clinical assessment and laboratory tests. Blood cultures are positive in up to 75% of cases, but real-time PCR increases diagnostic yield by >40%. Lumbar puncture is recommended unless contraindicated, with CSF analysis aiding in identifying meningococcal meningitis.
keyPoints: Elevations in WBC count and inflammatory markers (e.g., C-reactive protein) are suggestive of meningococcal disease.
Real-time PCR increases diagnostic yield by >40% and remains positive after antibiotic administration.
Lumbar puncture is crucial for suspected meningococcal meningitis unless contraindicated, with CSF analysis showing indistinguishable features from other bacterial meningitis.

items.title: Management of Meningococcal Shock and Intracranial Pressure
items.content: Urgent management of meningococcal infections should prioritize addressing hypovolemic shock (meningococcemia) and raised intracranial pressure (meningococcal meningitis). Airway patency is crucial if consciousness is impaired. Fluid resuscitation, oxygen therapy, intubation, and correction of metabolic derangements are essential steps.
keyPoints: Hypovolemic shock and raised intracranial pressure are critical issues in meningococcal disease management.
Airway patency may require intervention if consciousness is depressed due to shock or increased intracranial pressure.
Fluid resuscitation should be aggressive, with replacement of circulating volume several times; elective intubation recommended if shock persists after 40 mL/kg.
Metabolic derangements such as hypoglycemia and coagulopathy must be anticipated and corrected.

items.title: Empirical Antibiotic Therapy for Meningococcal Disease
items.content: Empirical antibiotic therapy should include third-generation cephalosporins like ceftriaxone or cefotaxime to cover potential penicillin-resistant bacteria. Vancomycin is often recommended in sepsis management. Penicillin can be used once resistance patterns are known.
keyPoints: Empirical therapy includes third-generation cephalosporins: ceftriaxone (75–100 mg/kg per day) or cefotaxime (200 mg/kg per day).
Vancomycin is recommended for sepsis and suspected bacterial meningitis.
Penicillin sensitivity in meningococcus ranges from 0.12–1.0 μg/mL; use after confirming resistance patterns.

items.title: Adjunctive Therapies and Treatment Duration for Meningococcal Disease
items.content: The duration of treatment for meningococcal meningitis or septicemia is typically 7 days, though shorter courses may be effective. Glucocorticoids are controversial as adjuncts; they show potential benefit in adults but not recommended in septicemia. Other therapies like HA1A and activated protein C have limited evidence.
keyPoints: Conventional treatment duration for meningitis or septicemia is 7 days, with shorter courses also effective.
Glucocorticoids may be beneficial in adult meningitis but are not recommended for septicemia.
Adjunctive therapies like HA1A and activated protein C lack sufficient evidence to recommend use.

items.title: Common Complications and Prognostic Factors in Meningococcal Disease
items.content: Meningococcal disease can lead to severe complications despite treatment. Common complications include scarring from necrotic skin lesions, hearing loss, neurological issues, and psychological disorders. Severe cases may require amputations due to tissue viability loss. Prognostic factors for poor outcomes include shock, age extremes, coma, purpura fulminans, coagulation disorders, metabolic acidosis, low protein levels, high PAI-1, and low inflammatory markers.
keyPoints: 10% mortality rate despite treatment
Scarring in 10% of cases; skin grafting may be needed
13% average skin surface area involvement
Amputations required in 1–2% of survivors
5% suffer hearing loss, 7% have neurological complications
21% report pain post-recovery
25% (MOSAIC study) experience psychological disorders
Prognostic factors: shock, age extremes, coma, purpura fulminans, coagulation issues, metabolic acidosis, low antithrombin/proteins S and C, high PAI-1, low ESR/CRP
GMSPS useful for severity assessment

items.title: Psychosocial Outcomes and Emergency Management in Meningococcal Disease
items.content: Survivors of meningococcal disease may face adverse psychosocial outcomes, including reduced quality of life and neurologic development issues. However, some studies do not confirm these findings. In severe cases like fulminant meningococcemia, rapid intervention is crucial to reduce mortality and prevent ischemic skin loss.
keyPoints: Adverse psychosocial outcomes reported: reduced quality of life, lowered self-esteem, neurologic development issues
Increased rates of ADHD and special educational needs in some studies
Fulminant meningococcemia has higher mortality/ischemic risk than meningitis
Priority is on recognizing compromised airways, breathing, or circulation for urgent intervention

items.title: Immunization as the Primary Prevention Strategy for Meningococcal Disease
items.content: Given the high mortality rates of meningococcal disease, immunization is crucial at a population level. Secondary prophylaxis with antibiotics is recommended for close contacts to prevent further spread.
keyPoints: Immunization is essential due to persistent high mortality despite intensive care improvements.
Secondary prophylaxis with antibiotics is recommended for household and 'kissing' contacts.

items.title: Limitations of Plain Polysaccharide Vaccines
items.content: Plain polysaccharide vaccines have limited efficacy, especially in early childhood and infants. They induce short-lived immunity and can lead to immunologic hyporesponsiveness upon booster doses.
keyPoints: Polysaccharide vaccines provide 50 μg per dose of each capsular group.
Immunogenicity is poor in early childhood; efficacy >90% only in young adults for group C.
Booster doses do not increase antibody levels and can cause immunologic hyporesponsiveness.

items.title: Advantages of Conjugate Vaccines Over Plain Polysaccharide Vaccines
items.content: Conjugate vaccines, by chemically linking polysaccharides to a carrier protein, overcome the limitations of plain polysaccharide vaccines. They generate a more robust and long-lasting immune response.
keyPoints: Chemical conjugation with proteins like CRM197 or tetanus toxoid enhances immunogenicity in infants.
Conjugate vaccines induce T cell-dependent responses, allowing for memory B-cell generation.
The UK's MenC vaccination campaign significantly reduced group C cases from 955 to 29 between 1998–1999 and 2011–2012.

items.title: Efficacy of Quadrivalent Conjugate Vaccines in Reducing Meningococcal Disease
items.content: Quadrivalent conjugate vaccines containing A, C, Y, and W polysaccharides have been highly effective in reducing meningococcal disease. In the UK, their use since 2015 has led to a significant decline in cases caused by these capsular groups.
keyPoints: Combined vaccine effectiveness of 94% against C, W, and Y disease
36% reduction in carriage of capsular groups C, W, and Y at 2 months postvaccination
Evidence of herd immunity with potential elimination over the coming decade

items.title: Impact of Monovalent Capsular Group A Vaccine in Sub-Saharan Africa
items.content: The introduction of a monovalent capsular group A vaccine has significantly controlled epidemic meningococcal disease in the sub-Saharan African meningitis belt, leading to over a 90% reduction in cases.
keyPoints: >90% reduction in disease in vaccinated populations
Part of WHO's global roadmap 'Defeating Meningitis by 2030'

items.title: Efficacy and Limitations of OMV Vaccines for Group B Meningococcus
items.content: OMV vaccines have shown efficacy in reducing group B meningococcal disease, particularly during clonal outbreaks. However, they produce strain-specific immune responses with limited cross-protection.
keyPoints: 53% reduction in incidence among 14- to 16-year-old schoolchildren using a Norwegian outbreak strain
>70% efficacy reported for local outbreak strains in Cuba and New Zealand
Best suited for clonal outbreaks due to limited cross-protection

items.title: Effectiveness and Recommendations for 4CMenB Vaccine
items.content: The 4CMenB vaccine has demonstrated effectiveness in reducing meningococcal disease incidence, particularly among infants. In the UK, a 75% reduction was observed in fully vaccinated age groups with high coverage (95%). The vaccine's effectiveness is 53% after two doses and increases to 59% following a booster at one year.
keyPoints: 4CMenB recommended for routine use in infants in the UK since May 2015
75% reduction in disease incidence among fully vaccinated age groups with 95% coverage
Vaccine effectiveness: 53% after two doses, 59% post-booster at one year

items.title: Cost-effectiveness and Herd Immunity of MenB Vaccines in Infants
items.content: The cost-effectiveness of the capsular group B vaccine for infants is borderline due to low carriage rates among this age group. The impact on herd immunity is minimal, as infants are not commonly colonized with capsular group B meningococci.
keyPoints: Cost-effectiveness of MenB vaccines in infants is borderline
Infants rarely carry capsular group B meningococci
Unlikely to induce significant herd immunity

items.title: Use and Effectiveness of MenB-fHBP Vaccine in Adolescents
items.content: The MenB-fHBP vaccine is licensed for adolescents aged 10–25 years in the US and Europe. It induces a strong immune response, with fourfold rises in bactericidal antibody titers in 50-92% of individuals. While not recommended for routine use, it can be administered to high-risk groups.
keyPoints: Licensed for adolescents aged 10–25 years
Induces fourfold rise in bactericidal antibodies in 50-92%
Not recommended for routine use but advised for high-risk individuals

items.title: Increased Risk for Close Contacts of Meningococcal Disease Patients
items.content: Close contacts (household and kissing) of individuals with meningococcal disease are at a significantly increased risk of developing secondary disease. This risk is up to 1000 times higher than that of the general population, especially within the first week following exposure.
keyPoints: Risk for close contacts: Up to 1000 times higher than the general population
Secondary cases follow as many as 3% of sporadic cases
One-fifth of secondary cases are co-primary cases
Highest risk period: Within the first week after exposure
30% of secondary cases occur in the first week

items.title: Temporal Risk Distribution for Secondary Meningococcal Cases
items.content: The risk of developing meningococcal disease among close contacts decreases rapidly over time but remains elevated above baseline for up to one year. The majority of secondary cases occur within the first two weeks following exposure.
keyPoints: Risk falls rapidly post-exposure but stays elevated for up to 1 year
30% of secondary cases in the first week
20% of secondary cases in the second week

items.subtopic: Meningococcal Infections | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY AND MICROBIOLOGY
items.question: Which of the following is NOT a common manifestation of Neisseria meningitidis infection?
items.explanation: Neisseria meningitidis causes bacterial, not viral, meningitis.
items.reference: Meningococcal Infections · Meningococcal Infections | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY AND MICROBIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Meningococcal Infections | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY AND MICROBIOLOGY
items.question: Which capsular group of N. meningitidis is associated with an unencapsulated variant?
items.explanation: Group D is identified as an unencapsulated variant of group C.
items.reference: Meningococcal Infections · Meningococcal Infections | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY AND MICROBIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Meningococcal Infections | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY AND MICROBIOLOGY
items.question: What percentage of N. meningitidis isolates lack the genes for capsule synthesis and assembly?
items.explanation: As many as 16% of isolates lack the genes for capsule synthesis and assembly.
items.reference: Meningococcal Infections · Meningococcal Infections | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY AND MICROBIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Meningococcal Infections | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY AND MICROBIOLOGY
items.question: Which method has superseded serology for meningococcal typing?
items.explanation: High-throughput antigen gene sequencing has superseded serology for meningococcal typing.
items.reference: Meningococcal Infections · Meningococcal Infections | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY AND MICROBIOLOGY

items.question: What percentage of meningococcal disease cases in sub-Saharan Africa are caused by capsular group A?
items.explanation: Capsular groups C and W now account for the majority of outbreaks, with 65% being caused by capsular group C.

items.question: Which capsular group was predominantly responsible for meningococcal disease in children under 5 years old in the United States from 2011 to 2021?
items.explanation: Capsular group B was predominant in children age <5 years during this period.

items.question: What was the impact of mass immunization against capsular group C meningococci in England and Wales beginning in 1999?
options: Shift to predominantly capsular group A
items.explanation: The mass immunization program resulted in a large impact against the disease, reducing its prevalence.

items.question: What was the trend of meningococcal disease rates in the United States from 1997 to 2021?
items.explanation: The rate fell significantly from 1.2 cases per 100,000 in 1997 to 0.06 cases per 100,000 in 2021.

items.question: Which capsular groups are targeted by adolescent immunization programs?
items.explanation: Adolescent immunization programs target capsular groups A, C, Y, and W.

items.question: What is the principal determinant of disease susceptibility for meningococcal infections?
items.explanation: The principal determinant of disease susceptibility is age.

items.question: Which genetic deficiency increases the risk of meningococcal disease by up to 600-fold?
items.explanation: Complement deficiency, especially in terminal complement components (C5–9), properdin, or factor D, increases the risk significantly.

items.question: Which factor is associated with an increased risk of meningococcal disease due to enhanced expression of adhesion molecules in the nasopharynx?
items.explanation: Factors like tobacco smoking, recent viral respiratory infections, and Mycoplasma species infection increase adhesion molecule expression.

items.question: Which factor is NOT mentioned as contributing to the rarity of meningococcal colonization in early childhood?
items.explanation: The passage does not mention high levels of circulating antibodies as a factor for the rarity of colonization in early childhood.

items.question: What is the primary role of the meningococcal capsule according to the passage?
options: Providing resistance to phagocytosis and preventing desiccation
items.explanation: The capsule provides resistance to phagocytosis and may be important in preventing desiccation during transmission.

items.question: Which of the following is a consequence of endothelial injury in meningococcal septicemia?
options: Loss of glycosaminoglycans leading to increased vascular permeability
items.explanation: Endothelial injury leads to loss of glycosaminoglycans, resulting in increased vascular permeability.

items.question: What mediates endothelial activation during meningococcal septicemia?
options: Direct bacterial invasion of endothelial cells
items.explanation: Soluble CD14-bound endotoxin acts as a mediator of endothelial activation.

items.question: Which cytokine-related response is associated with meningitis in meningococcal infections?
options: Release of a spectrum of cytokines similar to that seen in septicemia
No significant change in cytokine levels
items.explanation: The local inflammatory response with the release of a spectrum of cytokines similar to that seen in septicemia is associated with meningitis.

items.question: What clinical feature is often absent early in the illness but develops over hours in meningococcal disease?
items.explanation: A nonblanching rash is often absent early but develops over hours in meningococcal disease.

items.question: What percentage of children presenting with a petechial or purpuric rash have meningococcal disease?
items.explanation: Less than 10% of children with a petechial or purpuric rash are found to have meningococcal disease.

items.question: Which age group is most commonly affected by meningococcal pneumonia?
items.explanation: Meningococcal pneumonia appears most often to affect individuals >10 years of age.

items.question: Which condition is characterized by repeated episodes of petechial rash, fever, joint pain, features of arthritis, and splenomegaly?
items.explanation: Chronic meningococcemia presents with repeated episodes of petechial rash, fever, joint pain, features of arthritis, and splenomegaly.

items.question: What is the typical time frame for the development of postmeningococcal reactive disease after the onset of meningococcal disease?
items.explanation: Postmeningococcal reactive disease typically develops ~4–10 days after the onset of meningococcal disease.

items.question: Which laboratory finding is NOT commonly associated with severe meningococcal septicemia?
items.explanation: Severe meningococcal septicemia is commonly associated with hypokalemia, not hyperkalemia.

items.question: Which diagnostic test has a sensitivity of up to 80% for meningococcal meningitis?
items.explanation: Gram's staining is up to 80% sensitive for detecting meningococcal meningitis.

items.question: What is the most common cause of death in meningococcal disease?
items.explanation: Death from meningococcal disease is most commonly associated with hypovolemic shock.

items.question: What is the recommended initial empirical antibiotic therapy for suspected meningococcal disease?
options: A third-generation cephalosporin such as ceftriaxone or cefotaxime
items.explanation: Empirical antibiotic therapy for suspected meningococcal disease consists of a third-generation cephalosporin.

items.question: What is the recommended action if shock persists after volume resuscitation at 40 mL/kg in meningococcemia?
items.explanation: If shock persists after volume resuscitation at 40 mL/kg, elective intubation is recommended.

items.question: What adjunctive therapy has shown a trend toward lower mortality rates in meningococcal disease but failed to show definitive benefit due to inadequate study power?
items.explanation: Recombinant bactericidal/permeability-increasing protein showed trends toward lower mortality but lacked definitive benefit due to inadequate study power.

items.question: Which of the following complications occurs in approximately 10% of patients with meningococcal disease?
options: Scarring after necrosis of purpuric skin lesions
items.explanation: Scarring after necrosis of purpuric skin lesions is the most common complication, affecting about 10% of cases.

items.question: What percentage of survivors of meningococcal disease may require amputations due to loss of tissue viability?
items.explanation: Amputations are necessary in 1–2% of survivors because of a loss of tissue viability after peripheral ischemia or compartment syndromes.

items.question: Which factor is NOT associated with a poorer prognosis in meningococcal disease according to the passage?
options: Low plasma concentrations of antithrombin and proteins S and C
items.explanation: A low erythrocyte sedimentation rate is associated with a poorer prognosis, not a high one.

items.question: Which meningococcal polysaccharide vaccines are known for their short-lived immunity and potential immunologic hyporesponsiveness after booster doses?
options: Quadrivalent capsular group A, C, Y, and W vaccines
Bivalent capsular groups A and C vaccines
items.explanation: Plain meningococcal polysaccharide vaccines (bivalent for groups A and C) are known to have short-lived immunity and can induce immunologic hyporesponsiveness after booster doses.

items.question: Why is there no meningococcal capsular group B plain polysaccharide vaccine available?
options: It is not effective in preventing disease at all ages.
The polysaccharide is perceived as 'self' due to its expression on neural cells.
It has been largely superseded by protein-polysaccharide conjugate vaccines.
It causes severe adverse reactions in humans.
items.explanation: There is no capsular group B plain polysaccharide vaccine because the B polysaccharide is perceived as 'self' due to its expression on neural cells, making it non-immunogenic in humans.

items.question: What was a significant outcome of the mass vaccination campaign with capsular group C meningococcal conjugate vaccine (MenC) in the United Kingdom?
options: Reduction in capsular group C cases due to herd immunity
Introduction of booster doses for infants
items.explanation: The mass vaccination campaign with MenC led to a significant reduction in capsular group C cases, attributed to both direct protection and herd immunity.

items.question: Why do conjugate vaccines overcome the hyporesponsiveness seen with plain polysaccharide vaccines?
options: They induce a T cell-independent immune response.
They replenish the memory B-cell pool.
They are administered in early childhood.
They contain higher doses of polysaccharides.
items.explanation: Conjugate vaccines overcome hyporesponsiveness by inducing a T cell-dependent immune response that allows for antibody production and replenishment of the memory B-cell pool.

items.question: Which age group was initially recommended to receive the quadrivalent conjugate meningococcal vaccine in the United States?
options: All children under 11 years of age
Only individuals over 16 years of age
items.explanation: The vaccine was initially recommended for all children >11 years in the United States.

items.question: What is the efficacy rate of the quadrivalent conjugate meningococcal vaccine one year after vaccination according to U.S. data?
items.explanation: The efficacy rate is 82% in the first year after vaccination.

items.question: Which vaccine showed a >90% reduction in epidemic meningococcal disease in sub-Saharan Africa?
items.explanation: The monovalent capsular group A vaccine reduced disease by >90% in the region.

items.question: What is a characteristic of OMV vaccines based on subcapsular antigens?
options: They produce broad cross-protection against all strains.
They are best suited to clonal outbreaks.
They have no reported efficacy in clinical trials.
They are based on capsular polysaccharides.
items.explanation: OMV vaccines produce strain-specific immune responses, suitable for clonal outbreaks.

items.question: Which age group has the highest rates of capsular group B carriage, apart from infancy?
items.explanation: Rates of capsular group B carriage are higher among teenagers and young adults than at other ages, apart from infancy.

items.question: What was the reported reduction in meningococcal disease age groups fully eligible for vaccination after implementing routine use of 4CMenB in the UK?
items.explanation: A recent analysis reported a 75% reduction in age groups that were fully eligible for vaccination with high coverage rate of 95%.

items.question: In which country was the 4CMenB vaccine recommended for routine use starting from May 2015?
items.explanation: The 4CMenB vaccine was recommended for routine use in the United Kingdom for all infants born from May 2015 onward.

items.question: Which of the following vaccines is licensed for use in adolescents and can be administered with Tdap, human papillomavirus, and MenACWY vaccines?
items.explanation: The MenB-fHBP vaccine is licensed for use in adolescents and can be used with a range of vaccines routinely administered in adolescence, including Tdap.

items.question: What is the approximate risk increase for close contacts developing secondary meningococcal disease compared to the general population?
items.explanation: Close contacts are at up to 1000 times the rate of developing secondary disease compared to the general population.

items.question: What percentage of secondary meningococcal cases occur within the first week after the index case presentation?
items.explanation: 30% of secondary cases occur in the first week.

items.question: What proportion of secondary meningococcal cases are considered co-primary cases?
items.explanation: About one-fifth of secondary cases are actually co-primary cases.

items.subtopic: Meningococcal Infections | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY AND MICROBIOLOGY
items.statement: N. meningitidis is a gram-positive bacterium.
items.explanation: N. meningitidis is a gram-negative aerobic diplococcus.
items.reference: Meningococcal Infections · Meningococcal Infections | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY AND MICROBIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Meningococcal Infections | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY AND MICROBIOLOGY
items.statement: Capsule switching in N. meningitidis can occur.
items.explanation: N. meningitidis can acquire new genes, allowing capsule switching from one capsular group to another.
items.reference: Meningococcal Infections · Meningococcal Infections | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY AND MICROBIOLOGY

items.statement: Capsular group Y meningococcal disease rates have been consistently high across all age groups and ethnicities.
items.explanation: The increase in capsular group Y cases has been sharpest among Americans of black ethnicity, individuals over 30 years of age, and those living with HIV.

items.statement: Introduction of a MenB vaccine in the UK led to an increase in group B meningococcal cases.
items.explanation: The introduction of the MenB vaccine for infants in the United Kingdom in 2015 led to a significant reduction in group B cases.

items.statement: Complement deficiency is found in a large proportion of individuals with meningococcal disease.
items.explanation: Complement deficiency is found in only a very small proportion (0.3%) of individuals with meningococcal disease.

items.statement: The peak incidence of meningococcal disease occurs during adolescence and young adulthood.
items.explanation: There is a second peak of disease among adolescents and young adults (15–25 years) in Europe and North America.

items.statement: The meningococcal capsule is not important for the organism's survival during transmission.
items.explanation: The capsule provides resistance to phagocytosis and may prevent desiccation, aiding in survival during transmission.

items.statement: Invasion of the bloodstream by N. meningitidis occurs frequently soon after colonization.
items.explanation: Invasion through the mucosa into the bloodstream occurs rarely, usually within a few days.

items.statement: The rash in meningococcal disease is always present from the onset of symptoms.
items.explanation: The rash is often absent early in the illness and develops over hours.

items.statement: Meningococcal meningitis can present with signs such as headache, neck stiffness, and photophobia.
items.explanation: These are classic signs of bacterial meningitis including meningococcal meningitis.

items.statement: In suspected cases of meningococcal disease, blood cultures are positive in all patients.
items.explanation: Blood cultures are positive in up to 75% of cases; they may be negative if antibiotics were administered before hospitalization.

items.statement: Real-time PCR analysis can increase the diagnostic yield for meningococcal disease by more than 40%.
items.explanation: Real-time PCR analysis of whole-blood samples increases the diagnostic yield by >40%.

items.statement: Aggressive fluid resuscitation with unbuffered electrolyte solutions is recommended for all patients with meningococcal disease.
items.explanation: Aggressive fluid resuscitation with unbuffered electrolyte solutions was found to increase mortality in febrile African children.

items.statement: The use of glucocorticoids is recommended for adjunctive treatment of meningococcal septicemia.
items.explanation: Therapeutic doses of glucocorticoids are not recommended in meningococcal septicemia.

items.statement: Permanent renal replacement therapy is commonly needed in patients with meningococcal disease.
items.explanation: Permanent renal replacement therapy is rarely needed, although prerenal failure due to impaired renal perfusion may occur.

items.statement: The Glasgow Meningococcal Septicaemia Prognostic Score (GMSPS) directs clinicians to specific interventions.
items.explanation: Scoring systems like GMSPS do not direct clinicians to specific interventions; they are used for severity assessment.

items.statement: Plain polysaccharide vaccines are immunogenic in early childhood.
items.explanation: Plain polysaccharide vaccines generally are not immunogenic in early childhood due to incomplete maturation of the splenic marginal zone.

items.statement: Conjugate vaccines have a similar reactogenicity profile as plain polysaccharide vaccines.
items.explanation: The reactogenicity of conjugate vaccines is indeed similar to that of plain polysaccharide vaccines.

items.statement: Early reports of an increase in the risk of Guillain-Barré syndrome after immunization with the diphtheria conjugate vaccine have been substantiated.
items.explanation: Further observation has not substantiated the increased risk of Guillain-Barré syndrome.

items.statement: The use of A, C, W, and Y conjugate vaccine in the UK since 2015 led to a reduction in meningococcal disease caused by these capsular groups.
items.explanation: There was a large reduction in disease due to these capsular groups.

items.statement: An infant immunization program using the capsular group B vaccine is likely to provide additional value through induction of herd immunity.
items.explanation: It is unlikely that an infant immunization program will provide additional value through induction of herd immunity because infants are not commonly colonized with capsular group B meningococci.

items.statement: The MenB-fHBP vaccine has been used to control outbreaks in educational institutions in the United States.
items.explanation: MenB-fHBP has been used to control outbreaks of meningococcal disease in educational institutions in the United States.

items.statement: The risk of developing secondary meningococcal disease remains above baseline for up to 1 year after the index case.
items.explanation: The risk falls rapidly but remains above baseline for up to 1 year.

items.statement: Most secondary cases of meningococcal disease occur in the second week after the index case presentation.
items.explanation: 30% occur in the first week, and 20% in the second week.

items.subtopic: Meningococcal Infections | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY AND MICROBIOLOGY
items.assertion: Hyperinvasive lineages of N. meningitidis are associated with more than one capsular group.
items.reason: The genetic stability of these clones indicates they are well adapted to the nasopharyngeal environment and efficient transmission.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain why hyperinvasive lineages are associated with more than one capsular group.
items.reference: Meningococcal Infections · Meningococcal Infections | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY AND MICROBIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Meningococcal Infections | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY AND MICROBIOLOGY
items.assertion: The majority of invasive meningococcal disease cases are caused by capsular groups other than A, B, C, X, Y, and W.
items.reason: Capsule switching allows N. meningitidis to adapt to different hosts.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the majority of invasive cases are caused by capsular groups A, B, C, X, Y, and W.
items.reference: Meningococcal Infections · Meningococcal Infections | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY AND MICROBIOLOGY

items.assertion: Capsular group C meningococcal disease rates decreased significantly in the UK after mass immunization.
items.reason: The mass immunization program against capsular group C was undertaken beginning in 1999.
items.explanation: The reason correctly explains why there was a significant decrease in capsular group C meningococcal disease rates.

items.assertion: Capsular group B is the predominant cause of meningococcal infection in children under 5 years old in the US.
items.reason: This is due to a decline in capsular groups A and C caused by immunization programs.
items.explanation: While both assertion and reason are true, the reason does not explain why group B is predominant; it's more related to changes in epidemiology over time.

items.assertion: The rarity of meningococcal disease suggests that individual susceptibility may be important.
items.reason: Most cases of infection with N. meningitidis in developed countries are sporadic.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Complement deficiency is associated with capsular group B disease frequently.
items.reason: Individuals with complement deficiencies have a higher risk of meningococcal disease.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because complement deficiency is rarely associated with capsular group B disease.

items.assertion: Endothelial injury is central to many clinical features of meningococcal disease.
items.reason: It leads to increased vascular permeability, loss of thromboresistance, and intravascular coagulation.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: The severity of meningococcal disease is solely determined by the levels of endotoxin in the blood.
items.reason: Endotoxin binds cell-bound CD14 with TLR4 to initiate an inflammatory cascade.
items.explanation: While the reason is true, the assertion is false because severity is also related to the magnitude of the inflammatory response.

items.assertion: Meningococcal septicemia can progress rapidly to death.
items.reason: Early symptoms of meningococcal septicemia are nonspecific and resemble an influenza-like illness.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason is not the correct explanation for the rapid progression to death.

items.assertion: Meningococcal meningitis often presents with a petechial or purpuric rash.
items.reason: The rash is due to widespread endothelial necrosis and occlusion of small vessels in the dermis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the presence of the rash.

items.assertion: Lumbar puncture should generally be avoided in meningococcal septicemia.
items.reason: Positioning for the procedure may critically compromise the patient's circulation in hypovolemic shock.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why lumbar puncture should be avoided.

items.assertion: Urinary antigen testing is a reliable method for diagnosing meningococcal infection.
items.reason: Serologic testing for meningococcal infection has not been adequately studied.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because urinary antigen testing is insensitive; the reason is true but does not explain the assertion.

items.assertion: Cultures usually become sterile within 24 hours of initiation of appropriate antibiotic therapy.
items.reason: This rapid sterilization is due to the effectiveness of antibiotics against meningococcal bacteria.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Activated protein C is recommended for use in pediatric sepsis due to meningococcal disease.
items.reason: Trials found a survival benefit with activated protein C in adult sepsis trials.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because activated protein C showed no benefit and potential risks in pediatric sepsis.

items.assertion: Capsular group A meningococcal polysaccharides are effective in preventing disease at all ages.
items.reason: Two doses administered to children 3–18 months of age or a single dose to older individuals have a protective efficacy rate of >95%.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining why group A polysaccharides are effective across all ages.

items.assertion: The duration of protection from MenC vaccine is long when administered in early childhood.
items.reason: Data show that immunity after a dose given in adolescence appears to be more prolonged than when administered in early childhood.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the duration of protection is short when the MenC vaccine is administered in early childhood, necessitating booster doses.

items.assertion: The quadrivalent conjugate vaccine has direct protection and reduces carriage of meningococcal disease.
items.reason: It provided a combined vaccine effectiveness of 94% against C, W, and Y disease and reduced carriage by 36% at 2 months postvaccination.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining the assertion.

items.assertion: OMV vaccines have been developed to overcome the lack of immunogenicity of group B capsule.
items.reason: They contain capsular polysaccharides that are highly immunogenic.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false as OMVs do not contain capsular polysaccharides.

items.assertion: The cost-effectiveness of capsular group B vaccine in infant immunization programs is borderline.
items.reason: Infants are not commonly colonized with capsular group B meningococci, so the impact on total population burden will be small.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why the cost-effectiveness is borderline.

items.assertion: The benefit of the 4CMenB vaccine is likely to be via direct protection only.
items.reason: A large cluster-randomized trial in Australia found no effect on carriage of disease-causing meningococci.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why the benefit is likely to be via direct protection only.

items.assertion: The rate of secondary cases is highest during the week after presentation of the index case.
items.reason: About one-fifth of secondary cases are actually co-primary cases occurring soon after the primary case.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain why the rate is highest in the first week.
